title: 10.5 Community Ecology
expanded_description: An introduction to community ecology—the study of how different species interact within an ecosystem. This guide explains the major types of species interactions: competition (when species compete for resources), predation (one species eats another), symbiosis (close long-term interactions including mutualism, commensalism, and parasitism), and ecological succession (how communities change over time). It covers the competitive exclusion principle, niche partitioning, predator-prey dynamics, and the role of keystone species in maintaining community structure.

core_concept.principle: Community ecology is the study of interactions between species. These interactions include competition (both species harmed), predation (one benefits, one is harmed), mutualism (both benefit), commensalism (one benefits, the other unaffected), and parasitism (one benefits, one is harmed). These interactions shape community structure, species diversity, and ecosystem function. Ecological succession describes how communities change over time after disturbance, from pioneer species to climax communities.
core_concept.explanation:
A community is all the populations of different species living in the same area and interacting with each other. These interactions—who eats whom, who competes with whom, who helps whom—determine the structure of the community, which species can coexist, and how the community changes over time.

Major types of species interactions:
• Competition (-/-): Both species are harmed. Occurs when species compete for limited resources (food, water, space, light).
• Predation (+/-): One species benefits (predator), the other is harmed (prey). Includes carnivory, herbivory, and parasitism (sometimes classified separately).
• Mutualism (+/+): Both species benefit. Examples: pollination, nitrogen-fixing bacteria in legume roots, coral and zooxanthellae.
• Commensalism (+/0): One species benefits, the other is unaffected. Examples: barnacles on whales, birds nesting in trees.
• Parasitism (+/-): One species benefits (parasite), the other is harmed (host). Often considered a form of predation, but parasite usually doesn't kill host immediately.

Key concepts in community ecology:
• Competitive exclusion principle: Two species cannot coexist indefinitely on the same limiting resource. One will outcompete the other.
• Niche partitioning: Species evolve to use different resources, allowing coexistence (resource partitioning, habitat partitioning, temporal partitioning).
• Keystone species: A species that has a disproportionately large effect on community structure relative to its abundance. Removing a keystone species can cause dramatic changes.
• Ecological succession: The predictable change in community composition over time after a disturbance. Primary succession starts on bare rock; secondary succession starts where soil already exists.

Fun fact: The competitive exclusion principle was first demonstrated in laboratory experiments by G.F. Gause, who showed that two species of Paramecium (single-celled organisms) could not coexist when grown together—one always outcompeted the other. But in nature, many similar species coexist because of niche partitioning.

image.alt: A diagram showing different species interactions: Competition (two deer fighting), Predation (wolf chasing deer), Mutualism (bee on flower), Commensalism (bird nesting in tree), Parasitism (tick on dog). A second diagram showing ecological succession: bare rock → lichens → mosses → grasses → shrubs → trees → forest.
image.caption: Community ecology: Species interact through competition, predation, mutualism, commensalism, and parasitism. These interactions shape community structure, which changes over time through ecological succession.

sections.introduction:
No species lives in isolation. Every organism is part of a community—a network of interactions with other species. Some interactions are competitive (squirrels competing for acorns), some are predatory (fox eating a rabbit), some are cooperative (bees pollinating flowers). These interactions determine which species can live together, how many of each there are, and how the community responds to change.

Why study community ecology?
• Understand why some species are rare and others common.
• Predict how communities respond to disturbance (fire, logging, climate change).
• Manage ecosystems for conservation (protecting keystone species).
• Control invasive species (which often lack natural predators).

Key questions in community ecology:
• How do species coexist when they seem to compete for the same resources?
• Why do some ecosystems have hundreds of species and others only a few?
• What happens when a species is removed from a community?
• How do communities recover from disturbance?

In this section, we'll explore the major types of species interactions—competition, predation, mutualism, commensalism, and parasitism—and how these interactions structure communities. We'll also examine ecological succession, the process by which communities change over time.

Understanding community ecology is essential for conservation. When we protect a species, we're not just protecting that species—we're protecting its interactions with others. Removing a keystone species (like wolves or sea otters) can cause cascading effects throughout the community. And when we restore an ecosystem, we're not just planting trees—we're trying to restore the web of interactions that make the community functional.

introduction_image.alt: A diagram of a forest community showing trees, deer, wolves, birds, insects, fungi, and soil bacteria, with arrows showing interactions (competition, predation, mutualism). A caption: "No species lives alone."
introduction_image.caption: Communities are networks of interacting species. Understanding these interactions is key to ecology and conservation.

key_concept.definition: Community ecology examines interactions between species: competition (-/-), predation (+/-), mutualism (+/+), commensalism (+/0), and parasitism (+/-). These interactions determine community structure, species diversity, and ecosystem function. Ecological succession describes the predictable change in community composition over time.
key_concept.context:
These interactions shape communities in predictable ways:
• Competition leads to niche partitioning—species evolve to use different resources, allowing coexistence.
• Predation can regulate prey populations and increase diversity (by preventing competitive exclusion).
• Mutualism enables species to thrive in environments they couldn't survive alone.
• Parasitism can regulate host populations and drive evolution.
• Succession shows how communities recover from disturbance.

Real-world connection: The removal of sea otters from the Pacific coast (due to fur trade) caused a trophic cascade. Sea otters eat sea urchins. Without otters, urchins exploded, overgrazed kelp forests, and destroyed the habitat for many fish species. When otters were protected and returned, kelp forests recovered. This shows how a single species (keystone) can structure an entire community through its interactions (predation).

image.alt: A table of interaction types: Competition (-/-), Predation (+/-), Mutualism (+/+), Commensalism (+/0), Parasitism (+/-). Examples with icons: deer competing, wolf eating deer, bee on flower, bird in tree, tick on dog.
image.caption: Types of species interactions: Competition (both harmed), predation (one benefits, one harmed), mutualism (both benefit), commensalism (one benefits, other unaffected), parasitism (one benefits, one harmed).

sections.key_concept: Species interactions—competition, predation, mutualism, commensalism, and parasitism—determine community structure. Ecological succession describes how communities change over time, from pioneer species to climax communities.

classifications.value: Understanding how species compete, how they coexist, and the competitive exclusion principle.
characteristics: Competition: Occurs when species use the same limited resource (food, water, space, light, mates). Both species are harmed (-/-).
Intraspecific competition: Competition within the same species. This is a major density-dependent factor regulating populations.
Interspecific competition: Competition between different species. Can lead to competitive exclusion or niche partitioning.
Competitive exclusion principle: Two species cannot coexist indefinitely on the same limiting resource. One will outcompete the other.

Niche partitioning: Species evolve to use different resources, allowing coexistence. Types: resource partitioning (using different parts of resource), habitat partitioning (living in different areas), temporal partitioning (active at different times).
Character displacement: When competing species evolve different traits to reduce competition. Example: Darwin's finches—different beak sizes for different seed types.
classifications.behavior:
Competition is one of the most important forces shaping communities. When two species compete for the same resource, one of three things can happen:
1. Competitive exclusion: One species outcompetes the other, and the inferior species is eliminated from the area.
2. Niche partitioning: The species evolve to use different resources or habitats, allowing coexistence.
3. Local extinction: In some cases, both species may decline or one may go extinct.

Classic experiments by G.F. Gause showed competitive exclusion in laboratory populations of Paramecium. When grown separately, both species thrived. When grown together, one species always outcompeted the other.

In nature, we see many similar species coexisting—for example, 5-10 species of warbler in the same forest. How? Niche partitioning. Warblers feed in different parts of the trees (some high, some low, some outer branches, some inner) and eat different insects. This reduces competition.

Real-world connection: Invasive species often succeed because they are better competitors than native species. Zebra mussels in the Great Lakes outcompete native mussels for food and space. Purple loosestrife in wetlands outcompetes native plants. Understanding competition helps predict which invasive species will be problematic and how to manage them.

examples.explanation:
Darwin's finches on the Galapagos Islands show how competition drives evolution: (1) Different species: Several finch species live on the islands, each with different beak sizes. (2) Competition: When two species with similar beak sizes live on the same island, they compete for the same seeds. (3) Character displacement: Over time, the species evolve different beak sizes—one larger (for large seeds), one smaller (for small seeds). This reduces competition. (4) Evidence: On islands where only one species lives, its beak size is intermediate. On islands where two species live, their beak sizes diverge. (5) Mechanism: Natural selection favors individuals that can eat different seeds, reducing competition with the other species.

Why this matters: Darwin's finches are a classic example of how competition shapes evolution. They also show that competition doesn't always lead to exclusion—it can lead to adaptation that allows coexistence.

image.alt: A diagram showing different finch species with different beak sizes. A graph showing beak size distribution: on islands with one species (intermediate), on islands with two species (diverged). Photos of finches.
image.caption: Darwin's finches: Competition drives character displacement—beak sizes diverge when two species compete, reducing competition and allowing coexistence.

classifications.type: Predation: The Hunter and the Hunted
classifications.value: Understanding predator-prey dynamics, adaptations, and the role of predation in structuring communities.
characteristics: Predation: One species (predator) kills and eats another (prey). Predator benefits (+), prey is harmed (-).
Types: Carnivory (predator eats prey), herbivory (animal eats plants), parasitism (lives on/in host, often not immediately fatal).
Predator-prey cycles: Classic example—lynx and hare cycle every 9-11 years. Prey increase, predators increase, prey decline, predators decline, cycle repeats.

Adaptations: Predators evolve speed, stealth, camouflage, venom. Prey evolve camouflage, warning coloration (aposematism), mimicry, defensive structures (shells, spines), behavioral adaptations (hiding, flocking).

Keystone predators: Predators that have disproportionate effects on community structure. Examples: wolves in Yellowstone, sea otters (control sea urchins, protect kelp forests), starfish in intertidal zones.
Top-down control: Predators control prey populations, which controls lower trophic levels. Opposite of bottom-up control (resources control populations).
classifications.behavior:
Predation is a major force shaping communities. Predators control prey populations, which can prevent prey from overgrazing their food sources. This is called a trophic cascade.

Classic example: Yellowstone wolves. When wolves were removed, elk populations exploded and overgrazed willows and aspens. When wolves were reintroduced, elk numbers decreased, and elk behavior changed (they avoided stream areas). Willows and aspens recovered, beavers returned, and wetlands were restored.

Predator-prey cycles are common in nature. The lynx-hare cycle is one of the best-documented. Trapping records from Canada show regular 9-11 year cycles. When hares are abundant, lynx have plenty of food and increase. Lynx predation reduces hare population. With fewer hares, lynx starve or have fewer young; lynx population declines. With fewer lynx, hare population recovers. The cycle repeats.

Prey have evolved amazing adaptations to avoid predators:
• Camouflage: blending in with environment (moth on bark, stick insect).
• Warning coloration (aposematism): bright colors signal toxicity (poison dart frogs, monarch butterflies).
• Mimicry: harmless species mimic toxic ones (viceroy butterfly mimics monarch).
• Defensive structures: shells, spines, thorns.
• Behavioral adaptations: hiding, flocking (safety in numbers), alarm calls.

Real-world connection: Overfishing of sharks (top predators) has caused trophic cascades in marine ecosystems. With fewer sharks, rays and smaller sharks increase. They eat more scallops and clams, causing collapse of shellfish fisheries. This shows that predators aren't just "bad" for prey—they maintain balance in the ecosystem.

examples.process: Sea Otters, Urchins, and Kelp: A Trophic Cascade
examples.explanation:
Sea otters are a keystone predator in kelp forest ecosystems: (1) Normal state: Sea otters eat sea urchins. Urchins graze on kelp but are kept in check by otters. Kelp forests thrive, providing habitat for fish, invertebrates, and other species. (2) Fur trade: Sea otters were hunted nearly to extinction for their fur. Without otters, urchin populations exploded. (3) Overgrazing: Urchins overgrazed kelp forests, creating "urchin barrens"—areas with no kelp, few fish, low biodiversity. (4) Recovery: When otters were protected and returned, they ate the urchins. Kelp forests recovered, and biodiversity increased. (5) Current threats: Killer whales (orcas) have started eating otters (due to decline of their usual prey), causing urchin outbreaks in some areas.

Why this matters: The sea otter-urchin-kelp system shows how a single predator can structure an entire community. It's a classic example of a keystone species and a trophic cascade.

image.alt: A diagram showing two states: with sea otters (otters eat urchins, kelp forest healthy) and without otters (urchins explode, kelp forest destroyed, urchin barrens). Photos of a sea otter, sea urchin, and kelp forest.
image.caption: Sea otters are keystone predators. They eat sea urchins, which prevents urchins from overgrazing kelp forests. Without otters, urchins destroy the kelp forest—a trophic cascade.

classifications.value: Understanding close, long-term interactions between species: mutualism (both benefit), commensalism (one benefits, other unaffected), and parasitism (one benefits, other harmed).
characteristics: Mutualism (+/+): Both species benefit. Examples: Pollination (bee gets nectar, plant gets pollinated), nitrogen-fixing bacteria in legume roots (bacteria get food, plant gets nitrogen), mycorrhizae (fungi help plants absorb nutrients, fungi get sugars), coral and zooxanthellae (algae provide food, coral provides shelter), lichens (fungus and alga together).

Commensalism (+/0): One species benefits, the other is unaffected. Examples: Barnacles on whales (barnacles get transport and access to food, whale unaffected), birds nesting in trees (bird gets shelter, tree unaffected), remora fish attached to sharks (remora gets food scraps, shark unaffected).

Parasitism (+/-): One species benefits (parasite), the other is harmed (host). Types: ectoparasites (live on outside—ticks, fleas, lice), endoparasites (live inside—tapeworms, malaria parasite), brood parasites (cuckoo lays eggs in other birds' nests). Parasites often don't kill host immediately (unlike predators).

Coevolution: Species that interact closely (mutualists, predators-prey, parasites-hosts) often evolve in response to each other. Example: flowering plants and their pollinators evolve traits that match.
classifications.behavior:
Symbiosis means "living together." These close relationships are common in nature and often essential for the species involved.

Mutualism is everywhere. About 80% of land plants have mycorrhizal fungi associated with their roots—the fungi help the plant absorb water and nutrients, and the plant provides sugars to the fungi. Without these fungi, many plants would struggle to survive.

Pollination is another critical mutualism. Bees, butterflies, birds, and bats visit flowers for nectar, and in the process, transfer pollen. About 75% of flowering plants and 75% of global food crops depend on animal pollinators.

Parasitism is also common. Ticks, fleas, tapeworms, and the malaria parasite are all parasites. Parasites can regulate host populations and drive evolution—hosts evolve defenses, parasites evolve counter-defenses.

Commensalism is harder to prove—is the other species truly unaffected? Often, the distinction is subtle. For example, birds nesting in trees may slightly affect the tree (some branches break), but the effect is usually negligible.

Real-world connection: Mycorrhizal fungi are increasingly recognized as critical for ecosystem health. Soil disturbance (tilling, erosion) damages fungal networks. In restoration projects, inoculating soil with mycorrhizal fungi can help plants establish faster. Understanding this mutualism is important for agriculture, forestry, and restoration.

examples.process: Coral and Zooxanthellae: A Mutualism Under Threat
examples.explanation:
Coral reefs are built on a mutualism between coral animals and microscopic algae called zooxanthellae: (1) How it works: Zooxanthellae live inside coral tissues. They perform photosynthesis, producing sugars and oxygen that feed the coral. The coral provides shelter and nutrients (CO₂, nitrogen) for the algae. (2) Benefits: This mutualism allows corals to build reefs in nutrient-poor tropical waters. The algae give corals up to 90% of their energy. (3) Coral bleaching: When water temperatures rise (climate change), corals expel their zooxanthellae. The coral turns white (bleached) and may die if the algae don't return. (4) Impact: Mass bleaching events (2016, 2017, 2020) have damaged the Great Barrier Reef and reefs worldwide. (5) Recovery: If temperatures return to normal, corals can re-acquire algae and recover. But repeated bleaching can kill corals.

Why this matters: Coral bleaching shows how climate change threatens mutualisms. Without the algae, corals cannot survive. The loss of coral reefs threatens the 25% of marine species that depend on them, as well as fisheries, tourism, and coastal protection.

image.alt: A diagram showing healthy coral (with zooxanthellae) and bleached coral (without zooxanthellae). A graph of ocean temperature and bleaching events. Photos of healthy and bleached coral.
image.caption: Coral and zooxanthellae have a mutualism—algae provide food, coral provides shelter. When water warms, corals expel the algae (bleaching), and may die if the mutualism doesn't recover.

classifications.type: Ecological Succession: Community Change Over Time
classifications.value: Understanding how communities recover from disturbance, the stages of succession, and the role of pioneer species and climax communities.
characteristics: Ecological succession: The predictable change in community composition over time after a disturbance.

Primary succession: Starts on bare rock with no soil. Takes centuries. Stages: bare rock → lichens (pioneer species) → mosses → grasses → shrubs → trees → forest. Example: succession after volcanic eruption.

Secondary succession: Starts where soil already exists (after fire, flood, logging, farming). Faster than primary succession (decades to centuries). Stages: cleared land → weeds → grasses → shrubs → trees → forest.
Pioneer species: First species to colonize disturbed area. Adapted to harsh conditions. Examples: lichens (primary succession), weeds (secondary succession).
Climax community: Stable final stage of succession, determined by climate. In a given climate, succession tends toward the same community type (e.g., oak-hickory forest in eastern US).
Disturbance: Events that reset succession (fire, flood, storm, logging). Intermediate disturbance hypothesis: moderate disturbance can increase diversity.
classifications.behavior:
Ecological succession is the process of community change. After a disturbance (fire, flood, volcanic eruption, logging), the community goes through a series of stages. Early stages (pioneer species) are adapted to open, harsh conditions. Later stages (climax species) are adapted to stable conditions with competition for light and nutrients.

Primary succession is slow because soil must be formed. Lichens are often the first colonizers—they break down rock, and when they die, they add organic matter. Mosses and small plants follow, then grasses, then shrubs, then trees. Eventually, a forest develops. This process takes hundreds to thousands of years.

Secondary succession is faster because soil already exists. After a fire, weeds and grasses quickly colonize. Shrubs appear, then fast-growing trees (pioneer trees like aspen), then slower-growing climax trees (oak, maple, beech). A forest can recover in 50-150 years.

The climax community is determined by climate. In the eastern US, the climax is deciduous forest. In the Great Plains, it's grassland. In the desert, it's desert scrub. Fire can maintain some communities—for example, longleaf pine forests and oak savannas depend on periodic fire to prevent succession to forest.

Real-world connection: Mount St. Helens eruption (1980) created a natural laboratory for studying primary and secondary succession. The blast zone was covered in ash; some areas had soil (secondary succession), some had no soil (primary succession). Scientists have been studying the site for 40 years, documenting the return of plants, animals, and ecosystem function. This research helps us understand how ecosystems recover from disturbance.

examples.process: Mount St. Helens: Succession in Action
examples.explanation:
The 1980 eruption of Mount St. Helens created a unique opportunity to study succession: (1) Eruption: The volcanic blast destroyed forests, covered the landscape with ash, and created a 300-square-mile "blast zone." (2) Primary succession areas: Some areas were covered with thick ash and had no surviving soil or plants. Pioneer species (weeds, lupine) colonized first. Lupine, a nitrogen-fixing plant, helped build soil by adding nitrogen. (3) Secondary succession areas: Some areas had surviving soil and roots. Plants (fireweed, alder) grew back quickly from roots and seeds. (4) Animal recovery: Pocket gophers (which burrow) survived and played a key role by bringing soil to the surface and aerating the ash. (5) Current status: After 40 years, forests are regrowing, but recovery will take many more decades. Some areas still have few trees; others are approaching pre-eruption forest.

Why this matters: Mount St. Helens shows that succession is slow—especially primary succession. It also shows that species like lupine (nitrogen-fixers) and pocket gophers (soil engineers) can speed recovery.

image.alt: Photos of Mount St. Helens: before eruption (1980), after eruption (ash, dead trees), 10 years later (weeds, small plants), 40 years later (forest regrowing). A graph showing vegetation recovery over time.
image.caption: Mount St. Helens eruption (1980) created a natural laboratory for studying succession. After 40 years, forests are regrowing, but full recovery will take centuries.

principles.principle: The Competitive Exclusion Principle: No Two Species Can Occupy the Same Niche
principles.statement: Gause's competitive exclusion principle states that two species cannot coexist indefinitely on the same limiting resource. If two species compete for exactly the same resource, one will outcompete the other. Coexistence requires niche differentiation (resource partitioning).
principles.application:
This explains why:
• So many similar species exist in nature—they've evolved to use different resources.
• Invasive species often outcompete natives (they are better competitors or lack predators).
• Species diversity increases with habitat complexity (more niches available).
• Darwin's finches have different beak sizes for different seeds.

Everyday example: In a garden, you can't grow two types of plants that need exactly the same water, light, and nutrients in the same spot—they will compete, and one will do better. But you can grow them in different spots (spatial partitioning) or plant them at different times (temporal partitioning).
principles.example: The warbler niche partitioning study by Robert MacArthur showed how five species of warblers coexist in the same forest. They all eat insects, but they feed in different parts of the trees—some high, some low, some outer branches, some inner. They also forage at different times and use different hunting techniques. This niche partitioning allows them to coexist without competitive exclusion.
principles.analogy: Competition is like two restaurants on the same street. If they serve the exact same food at the same prices, one will go out of business. But if one serves breakfast and the other dinner, or one serves Italian and the other Chinese, they can coexist.

image.alt: A diagram of Gause's Paramecium experiment: two species grown separately (both thrive), grown together (one outcompetes the other). Warbler diagram: different species feeding in different parts of trees (niche partitioning).
image.caption: Competitive exclusion: Two species cannot coexist on the same limiting resource. Niche partitioning (like warblers feeding in different tree parts) allows coexistence.

principles.statement: Some species have effects on community structure that are much larger than their abundance would suggest. These are keystone species. Removing a keystone species can cause dramatic changes, including loss of biodiversity and ecosystem collapse.
principles.application:
This explains why:
• Wolves in Yellowstone affect willows, beavers, and streams.
• Sea otters affect urchins and kelp forests.
• Starfish in intertidal zones affect mussels and other species.
• Protecting keystone species is a conservation priority.

Everyday example: A keystone is the top stone in an arch—if you remove it, the whole arch collapses. A keystone species plays a similar role in its ecosystem.
principles.example: Robert Paine's starfish removal experiment in the intertidal zone showed the keystone species concept. He removed the starfish (Pisaster ochraceus) from a section of shore. Without starfish (which eat mussels), mussels overgrew the area, outcompeting other species. Species diversity dropped from 15 to 8. This showed that a single predator (the starfish) maintained diversity by preventing mussels from dominating.
principles.analogy: A keystone species is like the quarterback on a football team—one player can affect the entire game, even though they're just one of 11 on the field.

image.alt: A diagram of Paine's starfish experiment: with starfish (15 species), without starfish (mussels dominate, 8 species). Photos of starfish, mussels, and the intertidal zone.
image.caption: Keystone species: Removing the starfish (Pisaster) caused mussels to overgrow and reduced species diversity from 15 to 8.

principles.principle: Succession Leads to Predictable Community Change
principles.statement: After disturbance, communities change through predictable stages. Pioneer species (adapted to harsh conditions) are replaced by later successional species (adapted to competition). In a given climate, succession tends toward a stable climax community, though disturbance can reset the process.
principles.application:
This explains why:
• Abandoned farmland eventually becomes forest.
• After a fire, the forest recovers in stages.
• Some ecosystems depend on periodic disturbance (fire-dependent forests).
• Restoration projects can mimic natural succession.

Everyday example: If you stop mowing your lawn, you'll first see weeds, then grasses, then shrubs, then tree seedlings. Over decades, it will become a forest (if climate allows). That's succession.
principles.example: The Indiana Dunes succession studies by Henry Cowles (early ecologist) showed how plant communities change over time. Along the Lake Michigan shoreline, older dunes (farther from shore) have more established forests; younger dunes (near shore) have grasses and shrubs. By studying dunes of different ages, Cowles could reconstruct the sequence of succession. This was one of the first studies to document ecological succession.
principles.analogy: Succession is like a construction project. First, you clear the site (disturbance). Then you build the foundation (pioneer species). Then you add the frame (intermediate species). Finally, you finish with the roof and interior (climax community). Each stage prepares the way for the next.

image.alt: A diagram of succession stages: bare ground → weeds → grasses → shrubs → young trees → mature forest. A photo of Indiana Dunes showing different stages on different-aged dunes.
image.caption: Ecological succession: Communities change predictably over time. Pioneer species are replaced by later successional species, eventually reaching a climax community.

components.definition: Competition (intraspecific, interspecific), predation (predator-prey cycles, trophic cascades), mutualism (pollination, mycorrhizae), commensalism, parasitism. Niche (species' role in community), keystone species, pioneer species, climax community. Succession: primary, secondary.
components.equation:
Species diversity = richness (number of species) × evenness (relative abundance).
Succession time: primary succession: 1000+ years; secondary succession: 50-200 years (forest) or less (grassland).
components.interpretation: High species diversity often indicates a healthy, stable community. Low diversity can indicate disturbance, stress, or recent succession. Keystone species maintain diversity. Succession shows that communities are dynamic, not static.

image.alt: A summary diagram of community ecology: competition (two deer), predation (wolf), mutualism (bee), commensalism (bird nest), parasitism (tick). Succession stages. Keystone species icon. Diversity equation.
image.caption: Key components of community ecology: species interactions, succession, and keystone species shape community structure and diversity.

categories.description: Invasive species are non-native species that spread rapidly and cause harm to native communities. They often succeed because they lack natural predators (enemy release hypothesis) or are superior competitors.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
Why invasive species succeed: (1) Enemy release—no predators, parasites, or diseases in new range. (2) Superior competition—outcompete natives for resources. (3) Novel weapons—produce chemicals that inhibit natives (allelopathy). (4) High reproductive rate—r-selected traits. (5) Habitat disturbance—invasions often follow disturbance.

Impacts: Reduce native biodiversity, alter ecosystem function (fire regimes, nutrient cycling), cause economic damage (billions/year).

Examples: Zebra mussels (Great Lakes), kudzu (southeastern US), European starling (North America), cane toad (Australia), lionfish (Atlantic).

examples.process: Kudzu: The Vine That Ate the South
examples.explanation:
Kudzu is an invasive vine introduced to the US from Japan in 1876: (1) Introduction: Planted for erosion control and as ornamental. (2) Spread: With no natural predators or diseases, kudzu grows rapidly—up to a foot per day. It covers trees, buildings, and power lines. (3) Impacts: Smothers native plants, kills trees, reduces biodiversity, costs millions in control. (4) Why it succeeds: Fast growth, nitrogen fixation (enriches soil, helping itself), few herbivores. (5) Control: Grazing (goats eat it), herbicides, mowing—but it's extremely persistent.

Why this matters: Kudzu shows how a species introduced without its natural enemies can become a community-dominating pest. It's a classic example of invasion biology.

image.alt: A photo of kudzu covering trees, power lines, and buildings. A map showing kudzu spread in the southeastern US. A diagram of a goat eating kudzu (control method).
image.caption: Kudzu: An invasive vine that grows up to a foot per day, smothering native plants and trees. It has no natural predators in the US.

categories.description: Some communities are more stable (resistant to change) and resilient (recover quickly after disturbance) than others. Factors include species diversity, keystone species, and disturbance history.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
Stability: The ability to resist change. Diverse communities are often more stable because if one species declines, others can fill its role (functional redundancy).

Resilience: The ability to recover after disturbance. Communities with high resilience can bounce back quickly after fire, flood, or other disturbance.

Factors affecting stability: Species diversity, keystone species presence, connectivity (corridors), stress levels.

Ecosystem services: Stable, resilient communities provide reliable ecosystem services—clean water, pollination, carbon storage.

examples.explanation:
Coral reefs are among the most diverse ecosystems on Earth—and also among the most threatened: (1) Biodiversity: Reefs cover <1% of ocean floor but support 25% of marine species. High diversity provides functional redundancy—many species perform similar roles. (2) Resilience: Healthy, diverse reefs can recover from bleaching, storms, and other disturbances. But repeated stressors reduce resilience. (3) Loss of resilience: Overfishing removes herbivorous fish, allowing algae to overgrow corals after bleaching. Without herbivores, reefs may not recover—they shift from coral-dominated to algae-dominated. (4) Managing for resilience: Protecting herbivorous fish, reducing local stressors (pollution, overfishing), and reducing carbon emissions (to slow warming) can help reefs retain resilience.

Why this matters: Coral reefs show that biodiversity is not just about counting species—it's about ecosystem function and resilience. Losing species can tip a community into an alternative, less desirable state (coral to algae).

image.alt: A diagram showing coral reef before and after: healthy reef (coral, fish, herbivores) → bleaching → with herbivores (recovery) vs. without herbivores (algae takeover). Photos of healthy reef and algae-dominated reef.
image.caption: Coral reef resilience: Diverse reefs with herbivorous fish can recover from bleaching. Without herbivores, algae take over and the reef may not recover.

sections.section_title: Worked Example: Community Ecology Case Analysis

worked_examples.title: Case Study: Sea Otter Decline in Alaska
worked_examples.problem: In the Aleutian Islands, sea otter populations have declined by 90% since the 1990s. Scientists observed that sea urchin populations increased, and kelp forests declined. The decline in otters coincided with an increase in killer whale sightings. (1) What type of interaction is at play? (2) Explain the cascade of effects. (3) What would you expect to happen to fish populations? (4) How is this an example of a keystone species?

step_by_step.calculation: Killer whales eat sea otters → predation (-/+). Sea otters eat sea urchins → predation (-/+). Sea urchins eat kelp → herbivory (-/+). This is a three-level trophic cascade.

Normal: Otters control urchins; urchins control kelp; kelp forests thrive. After killer whales start eating otters: Otter population declines → urchins increase → urchins overgraze kelp → kelp forests decline. This is a top-down cascade (predator removal affects lower levels).
Kelp forests provide habitat for fish (shelter, food). When kelp declines, fish populations likely decline as well. This affects fisheries and other predators.

Sea otters are a keystone species—their effect on the community is much larger than their abundance. Removing otters (or reducing them) caused a dramatic shift from kelp forest to urchin barrens. Their presence maintains the entire community structure.

worked_examples.final_answer: (1) Interactions: killer whale-otter (predation), otter-urchin (predation), urchin-kelp (herbivory). (2) Cascade: fewer otters → more urchins → less kelp → loss of kelp forest habitat. (3) Fish populations likely decline (loss of habitat). (4) Sea otters are keystone species—their presence maintains kelp forests and high biodiversity; their decline shifts the community to urchin barrens with low diversity.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Understanding predator-prey interactions, trophic cascades, and keystone species explains how changes at one level ripple through the community.

sections.introduction: Follow the process of secondary succession after a forest fire, from bare ground to mature forest.

process_flow.description: A forest fire burns the area, killing trees but leaving soil intact. Seeds, roots, and soil organisms survive.
process_flow.mechanism: Fire removes above-ground vegetation but doesn't destroy the soil seed bank or root systems. Some species (fire-adapted pines) depend on fire for seed release.

image.alt: A forest fire. After the fire: bare ground, charred trees, but soil remains.
image.caption: Step 1: Disturbance (fire). Vegetation is removed, but soil and roots remain—this is secondary succession.

process_flow.description: Weeds, grasses, and fast-growing plants (fireweed) quickly colonize the burned area.
process_flow.mechanism: These pioneer species have seeds that survive fire or disperse quickly. They grow rapidly in full sunlight.
process_flow.result: Weeds and grasses cover the area

image.alt: Bare ground with fireweed and grasses growing. Fireweed in flower.
image.caption: Step 2: Pioneer species. Weeds and fireweed colonize first, taking advantage of full sunlight and open conditions.

process_flow.description: Shrubs (blackberry, raspberry, alder) grow among the grasses. They create shade, which shades out some grasses.
process_flow.mechanism: Shrubs have deeper roots and can compete with grasses. They also improve soil (alder fixes nitrogen).

image.alt: Grasses and shrubs growing. Alder trees (nitrogen-fixing) are visible. Shade from shrubs is beginning to reduce grasses.
image.caption: Step 3: Shrubs appear. Shrubs (like alder) shade out grasses and improve soil, preparing the way for trees.

process_flow.description: Fast-growing trees (aspen, birch, pine) grow up through the shrubs. They create shade, which kills shade-intolerant shrubs.
process_flow.mechanism: Pioneer trees have seeds that disperse widely and grow rapidly in full sun. They eventually shade out the shrubs.

image.alt: Aspen and birch trees growing above shrubs. Shrubs are dying back due to shade. Sunlight reaching the forest floor is reduced.
image.caption: Step 4: Pioneer trees (aspen, birch) grow rapidly, creating shade that kills shade-intolerant shrubs.

process_flow.description: Slower-growing, shade-tolerant trees (oak, maple, beech) grow beneath the pioneer trees. Eventually, they replace the pioneer trees.
process_flow.mechanism: Climax species seedlings can survive in shade. As pioneer trees die, climax trees take their place, creating a stable forest.

image.alt: Mature oak-maple forest with tall trees, shaded understory, and diverse plant and animal life.
image.caption: Step 5: Climax forest. Shade-tolerant trees (oak, maple) replace pioneer trees, creating a stable, diverse forest community.

sections.definition: These are more advanced concepts in community ecology—from island biogeography to ecological networks to alternative stable states.

process_types.type: Island Biogeography: Why Islands Have Fewer Species
process_types.energy_profile: The theory of island biogeography (MacArthur and Wilson) explains why islands have fewer species than mainlands, and why larger islands closer to mainlands have more species.
process_types.direction: Equilibrium theory: The number of species on an island is determined by the balance between immigration (new species arriving) and extinction (species dying out). Larger islands have lower extinction rates (more area, more resources). Islands closer to mainland have higher immigration rates (easier to reach). The equilibrium point (where immigration = extinction) determines species number.
process_types.function: Island biogeography theory has been applied to conservation—habitat fragments are like islands in a "sea" of development. Protecting larger fragments and maintaining corridors (connecting fragments) increases species persistence.
goals: Understand why islands have fewer species
Learn about habitat fragmentation and conservation

primary_example.explanation:
As forests are cleared for agriculture and development, remaining forest patches become "islands" in a sea of human-altered land: (1) Problem: Small, isolated forest patches lose species over time. Birds, large mammals, and forest-interior species are most vulnerable. (2) Predictions from island biogeography: Larger patches (lower extinction) and patches closer to other patches (higher immigration) will retain more species. (3) Evidence: Studies in the Amazon (Biological Dynamics of Forest Fragments Project) show that small, isolated fragments lose species rapidly. Large predators disappear first; then understory birds; then tree species decline. (4) Solutions: Protect large fragments; maintain corridors to connect fragments; restore habitat around fragments. (5) Importance: Island biogeography theory guides conservation planning—it's not enough to set aside land; size, shape, and connectivity matter.

Why this matters: Island biogeography theory shows that protected areas are like islands. To preserve biodiversity, we need large, connected reserves, not just small, isolated patches.

image.alt: A diagram showing a forest being cleared into fragments. Species richness vs. fragment size graph (larger fragments have more species). Corridors connecting fragments. Satellite image of Amazon deforestation.
image.caption: Habitat fragmentation creates "islands" of forest. Island biogeography theory predicts that larger, connected fragments will retain more species.

image.alt: A graph showing species number on islands: immigration rate (decreases with distance), extinction rate (decreases with area), equilibrium species number. Large, close islands have more species; small, far islands have fewer.
image.caption: Island biogeography: Species number on islands is determined by balance between immigration (distance) and extinction (area). This applies to habitat fragments too.

process_types.type: Alternative Stable States: When Communities Flip
process_types.energy_profile: Some ecosystems can exist in two or more different states (e.g., coral reef vs. algae reef, clear lake vs. turbid lake). Once they flip to an alternative state, they may not return without major intervention.
process_types.direction: Alternative stable states occur when positive feedback loops maintain the new state. For example, in a clear lake, aquatic plants stabilize sediments. If nutrient pollution causes an algal bloom, the plants die, sediments become unstable, and the lake stays turbid—even if nutrients are reduced. The lake is "stuck" in the turbid state. Returning to the clear state requires additional intervention (restoring plants).
process_types.function: Understanding alternative stable states is critical for restoration. If an ecosystem has flipped, simply reducing stressors may not be enough—active restoration may be needed to push it back.
goals: Understand that ecosystems can have tipping points
Learn why some ecosystems don't recover after stress is reduced

primary_example.explanation:
Coral reefs can exist in two stable states: coral-dominated (high biodiversity) or algae-dominated (low biodiversity): (1) Coral-dominated state: Corals build reef structure; herbivorous fish eat algae, keeping it from overgrowing corals. (2) Stressors: Overfishing removes herbivorous fish; bleaching kills corals; nutrient pollution fertilizes algae. (3) Flip to algae-dominated: Once algae cover the reef, coral larvae can't settle. Herbivores are gone or can't keep up. The reef shifts to algae-dominated. (4) Stuck: Even if overfishing stops and water quality improves, the reef may not return to coral because algae already dominate and coral larvae have nowhere to settle. (5) Restoration: Active restoration (outplanting corals, controlling algae, restoring herbivores) may be needed to flip it back.

Why this matters: Coral reefs show that ecosystems can have tipping points. Once they cross a threshold, recovery isn't automatic. This is why preventing degradation is easier than restoring after collapse.

image.alt: A diagram showing two stable states: coral reef (coral, fish, high diversity) and algae reef (algae, few fish, low diversity). A "ball in a valley" diagram showing two valleys (stable states) separated by a hill (threshold).
image.caption: Alternative stable states: Coral reefs can be coral-dominated or algae-dominated. Once they flip to algae, they may not return without active restoration.

image.alt: A graph showing ecosystem state vs. stressor (e.g., nutrient level). Two stable states exist over a range. A threshold where the system flips. The path back is different (hysteresis).
image.caption: Alternative stable states: Ecosystems can have tipping points. Once they cross a threshold, they may not return to the original state even if stressors are reduced (hysteresis).

sections.relationship: Community ecology is the study of interactions between species—competition, predation, mutualism, commensalism, parasitism—and how these interactions shape communities. Competition leads to niche partitioning and character displacement, allowing species to coexist. Predation can regulate populations and cause trophic cascades; keystone predators maintain diversity. Mutualisms (pollination, mycorrhizae) are essential for ecosystem function. Parasitism and disease can regulate host populations. Ecological succession describes how communities change over time after disturbance, from pioneer species to climax communities. Human activities—habitat fragmentation, invasive species, overfishing, climate change—are disrupting communities, causing species loss and ecosystem shifts. Understanding community ecology is essential for conservation, restoration, and managing the ecosystems that sustain us.

summary:
Community ecology is the study of interactions between species and how these interactions shape communities. A community is all the populations of different species living in the same area.

Types of species interactions:
• Competition (-/-): Both species harmed. Leads to competitive exclusion or niche partitioning. Character displacement (Darwin's finches) reduces competition.
• Predation (+/-): One benefits, one harmed. Includes carnivory, herbivory. Predator-prey cycles (lynx-hare). Keystone predators (wolves, sea otters, starfish) maintain diversity by controlling prey.
• Mutualism (+/+): Both benefit. Examples: pollination, mycorrhizae, nitrogen-fixing bacteria, coral-zooxanthellae.
• Commensalism (+/0): One benefits, other unaffected. Examples: barnacles on whales, birds nesting in trees.
• Parasitism (+/-): One benefits, one harmed. Ticks, tapeworms, malaria.

Keystone species:
Species with disproportionate effects on community structure. Examples:
• Sea otters control urchins, maintain kelp forests.
• Wolves in Yellowstone—trophic cascade to willows, beavers, streams.
• Starfish (Pisaster) in intertidal—prevents mussels from dominating.

Ecological succession:
• Primary succession: Starts on bare rock, no soil. Lichens → mosses → grasses → shrubs → trees → forest. Takes centuries.
• Secondary succession: Starts where soil exists (after fire, flood, farming). Weeds → grasses → shrubs → pioneer trees → climax forest. Takes decades to centuries.
• Pioneer species: First to colonize (r-selected).
• Climax community: Stable final stage, determined by climate.

Key principles:
• Competitive exclusion principle: No two species can occupy the same niche indefinitely. Coexistence requires niche partitioning.
• Keystone species: Some species have effects far beyond their abundance. Removing them causes cascading changes.
• Succession is predictable: Communities change through predictable stages after disturbance.

Human impacts:
• Invasive species: Lack natural predators, outcompete natives (kudzu, zebra mussels).
• Habitat fragmentation: Creates "islands" of habitat; smaller fragments lose species (island biogeography).
• Overfishing: Removes keystone predators, causing trophic cascades.
• Climate change: Threatens mutualisms (coral bleaching), shifts species ranges.

Real-world examples:
• Darwin's finches: Character displacement—beak sizes diverge when species compete.
• Lynx-hare cycle: 9-11 year predator-prey cycles.
• Sea otters: Keystone species—control urchins, maintain kelp forests.
• Coral bleaching: Climate change disrupts coral-zooxanthellae mutualism.
• Mount St. Helens: Natural laboratory for succession.

Understanding community ecology—competition, predation, mutualism, succession—is essential for conservation, restoration, and managing the ecosystems that support life on Earth.
